title: Lucerne
prose: GENERAL Lucerne can be cultivated in a wide range of climatic conditions and therefore different dormancy classes have been developed, namely from Class 1 (strongly dormant in winter, therefore non-active) to Class 10 (non-dormant, therefore winter active). Dormancy 11 is also available but is only suitable for extremely high-potential conditions. In South Africa, the best adjusted classes for the local climatic regions are classified as 5 (semi-dormant), 6 and 7 (intermediary dormant), 8 (non-dormant) and 9+ (strongly non-dormant). The stronger dormant types have a longer production lifespan and are better suited for grazing. In contrast, the non-dormant varieties have a shorter production lifespan and are better suited for hay production. A general rule is that a lucerne plant produces approximately 120 tons of dry matter over its life cycle. For this reason the dormant type is much more perennial than the non-dormant types that produce more hay per annum. A vigorous stand of lucerne is the basis for profitable production. For hay production the goal is production of 20 tons ha -1 per annum. PHYSICAL SOIL REQUIREMENTS AND CULTIVATION BEFORE PLANTING Lucerne does not grow in waterlogged conditions and needs well-drained soil for optimal production. Soils should be deep enough (at least 1.2 m) for storing adequate quantities of water. Clay content must preferably be lower than 35%. Soil preparation for establishing lucerne must preferably commence during the spring before the contemplated autumn planting. If a cash crop is fitted in before planting lucerne, at least two months must elapse after harvesting the cash crop before lucerne is planted in order to allow complete breakdown of crop residues. Fields that have been utilised for a number of seasons, must be deep-ploughed to break up plough soles. The most important considerations for ploughing are to promote root penetration, drainage and soil aeration and to neutralise soil compaction. Research has shown that soil compaction meaningfully reduces the yield, as well as the K content of tissue. Early preparation of fields is recommended in the case of a weed problem. It is essential to cultivate the field in spring and to allow it to lie fallow in summer and then again cultivate it in late summer and/or before establishing in autumn. If the soil pH is correct, a chemical fallow period (spray herbicide) can be considered as an alternative. The following options are therefore crucial, namely (a) a deep, well-drained soil, (b) the correct soil preparation and (c) the correct fertilizer supplement before and with establishment. CHEMICAL SOIL REQUIREMENTS Soil pH (H 2 O) between 6.5 and 7 is ideal. Before establishment on sandy soils, extractable Bray-1 phosphate (P) should be more than 40 mg kg -1 , 30 to 40 mg kg -1 on medium-textured soils and 20 to 25 mg kg -1 on clayey soils. Sufficient P is necessary for seedling growth, root growth and yield. The potassium analysis (K; ammonium acetate) should be more than 120 mg kg -1 on soils with a CEC of less than 6 cmol c kg -1 soil to ensure healthy seedlings and cold resistance (survival during the winter). Sufficient calcium (Ca), similar to K, is needed for healthy and strong root development and cold resistance. Sufficient magnesium (Mg) is crucial for photosynthesis. Sufficient sulphur (S) is needed for protein synthesis, whereas molybdenum (Mo) is essential for nitrogen fixation. Particularly in view of the high prices of fertilizers, it is important to take soil samples of top and subsoil and have them analysed by a reliable analytical laboratory before establishment. A soil sample should be taken several months before establishment to ensure that there is enough time for report back regarding analysis results so that lime and fertilizer can be ordered and delivered timeously. In addition, the lime should be worked in deeply well before establishment. FERTILIZATION BEFORE ESTABLISHMENT Soil cultivation before establishment is the last opportunity to mix relatively immobile fertilizers, such as lime, well with the soil so that chemical reactions can release elements that are available for uptake by the plant. Nutrients that should be supplemented, excluding lime, include P, K and S. Lime (comprises Ca and Mg) Liming of acid soil is an extremely important fertilizer correction and soil correction factor for the establishment and preservation of high-yield and high-quality lucerne stands and should be implemented before establishment. Guidelines for liming should take account of the exchangeable cations of the soil and not only the pH and the texture of the soil. Agricultural lime should be broadcast on the ground surface, and then, firstly, worked in with a disc (disc plough) and then, secondly, ploughed under deeply for maximum distribution and neutralisation of acids in the tillage layer (not less than 150 to 200 mm). Ploughing without discing can deposit lime in a layer at the plough sole. When large quantities of lime (> 6 tons ha -1 ) have to be applied, it is better to apply half before field preparation commences. The other half can be worked in either after ploughing or during subsequent field preparation. There are various liming media, namely calcitic (Ca-based neutralisers) and dolomitic (both Ca and Mg neutralisers) types. The soil analysis will determine the type of lime most suitable for application. Because lime reacts slowly with soil acids, it must be worked into the soil at least 3 to 12 months before establishment. Advantages of liming for lucerne include (a) creating favourable conditions for the availability of other elements, (b) improved establishment of the stand, (c) allowing longer production by the stand, (d) increases activity of nitrogen-fixing Rhizobium bacteria, (e) provide Ca and Mg (f) improved soil texture and soil cultivation, (g) increases availability of P and Mo, and (h) reduces Mn, Fe and aluminium (Al) toxicity. Phosphorus (P) Sufficient P in the soil increases the establishment success in that it promotes root growth and is important for flowering and seed production. Phosphorous fertilization is determined by soil analysis, yield target and clay quality of the soil. Phosphorus is immobile in most soil types. Phosphorus is provided to the plant through the process of diffusion, which implies that uptake occurs over very short distances (less than 2 cm) and therefore high P levels, as well as a well-developed root system, are very important. As P is taken up by the roots, a weakening in the concentration of P develops in the area surrounding the root. This is a passive process and no metabolic energy is needed. Research has shown that in soils with low to medium levels of P, P corrections before planting, while soil preparation is under way, are up to twice as effective as P that is applied after planting (topdressing). Phosphorus can also be applied by scattering a 12 to 25 mm strip next to or under the seed but it is more often strewn and then deeply (200 mm) worked into the soil shortly before planting. A minimum of 10 kg ha -1 is applied to soils with a high P quality. Rock phosphate is not recommended for lucerne because the pH at which rock phosphate is effective, is too low for lucerne production. In practice, minor differences between the use of liquid P fertilizer compared to P granular fertilizer, or the use of polyphosphates compared to orthophosphates, as P source for lucerne were observed. Because the availability of single-carrier P fertilizer is sometimes limited, ammonium phosphates seem to be successful. Guidelines for phosphorous fertilization are given in Table 5.12.2.1. Potassium (K) Potassium fertilization is determined by the soil analysis and the yield target. Although K has little influence on establishment, the yield and the survival of the stand are highly dependent on sufficient K. If soil analyses show medium or low K levels, sufficient K should be applied to provide for the needs of the lucerne. The correct quantity of K must be disced into the seedbed shortly before planting. Potassium must never be strewn in strips too close to the seed. Table 5.12.2.1. Guidelines for phosphorus fertilization Guidelines for K fertilization on soils with a CEC less than 6 cmol c kg -1 soil are given in Table 5.12.2.2, while K guidelines on soils with a CEC more than 6 cmol c kg -1 soil are given in Table 5.12.2.3. Potassium is one of the more mobile elements in the soil and on sandy soils in particular it must be supplemented regularly as topdressing. A sign of K deficiency is small necrotic spots on leaf margins. On sandy soils (< 15% clay) an additional maximum of 100 kg ha -1 K should be applied with establishment. Table 5.12.2.2. Guidelines for potassium fertilization in terms of soil analyses on soils with a CEC less than 6 cmol c kg -1 Table 5.12.2.3. Guidelines for potassium fertilization in terms of soil analyses on soils with a CEC greater than 6 cmol c kg -1 Sulphur (S) Legumes have a very high protein content and therefore a high S need. If necessary, elementary S can be used as an S source during establishment. However, elementary S must be converted to sulphate sulphur before it can be used by the plant. This process is relatively slow, especially if sulphur is applied by means of topdressing. The correct quantity of S can be supplemented with single superphosphate or potassium sulphate. Nitrogen (N) Research has shown that small supplements of N can improve establishment and yield in the first year. Table 5.12.2.4 shows the quantities of N that can be applied with establishment. Inoculation with Rhizobium bacteria is essential. Table 5.12.2.4. Quantities of nitrogen that can be applied in the first year with establishment MAINTENANCE FERTILIZATION Compared with other crops, lucerne has a high need of nutrients as it is a high-production crop with a high nutrient value. In Table 5.12.2.5 the quantities of nutrients extracted from the soil per ton of dry matter are given. Table 5.12.2.5. Nutrient quantities extracted from the soil by lucerne per ton of dry matter The above nutrients should be supplemented because the production and the nutritional value of the lucerne can reduce in the course of time. Trace elements such as boron (B), manganese (Mn), iron (Fe), zinc (Zn), copper (Cu) and molybdenum (Mo) are removed to a lesser extent but nevertheless play an essential role and shortages can cause huge losses. Because many of the nutrients that are removed come from the soil, it is not recommended that a fertilizer programme be based on the extraction of nutrients from the soil. A fertilizer programme should rather be based on soil analyses because they are the most reliable methods for preventing nutrient deficiencies. Observable symptoms can be used to help determine nutrient requirements for subsequent yield. By the time visual symptoms are present on the crop, the nutrient deficiency could have progressed so far that serious production losses and weakening stand can already manifest. Such symptoms can also reflect environmental conditions, impeded root growth, diseases or other problems unrelated to nutrient deficiencies. Plant tissue analyses can determine the nutritional status of the crop before any symptoms appear. These methods do not measure the nutrient levels to make a fertilization recommendation but if they are combined with a soil analysis, a complete fertilizer management programme can be compiled. Tissue tests can also be used to determine sulphur and microelement levels. In Table 5.12.2.6 norms for tissue analyses for different growth stages, namely early budding, late budding and flowering stages (1st flowering) are given. In all cases the top 150–200 mm of the plant is removed for the analysis. The total quantity of material needed for tissue analyses can be confirmed by the laboratory. Primary elements Nitrogen Lucerne obtains sufficient N from the symbiotic relationship with specific N-binding Rhizobium bacteria and from organic matter in the soil that releases N while it is decomposing. Topdressing with N on established stands that have healthy active buds, does not improve yield, quality or vitality of the stand; on the contrary, it can cause lower yield or quality due to the stimulation of the growth of grasses and weeds. Yield responses due to N fertilization can be expected on lucerne when the buds are inactive. The advantages of N addition can therefore be more during subsequent periods of the lucerne’s life. If it is applied as part of other fertilizers, N application should not exceed 45 kg ha -1 . Phosphorus and Potassium Lucerne needs large quantities of P and K and their role must be seen in synergy. Sufficient P is necessary for successful establishment and good root development, while K is essential for improved resistance against certain diseases, increased winter survival and persistency; therefore increased yield. Lucerne absorbs most nutrients, including P and K, from the top 150 to 200 mm of soil. Application of P and K should be based on recommendations following on a soil analysis. Phosphate is relatively immobile when added to the soil. It reacts and forms iron and aluminium complexes on more acidic soils (pH (H 2 O) < 5.5) or Ca complexes on high pH soils (pH (H 2 O) > 7.5) that reduce availability. Because P is not mobile, lucerne reacts better to it if it is applied with establishment rather than when the P is used as topdressing. If applications are necessary, they must only be applied in spring so that they can be made available during the growing season. Potassium, on the other hand, can leach from sandy soils and organic soils (peat or muck soils) and special precautions must be taken in managing such conditions. Table 5.12.2.6. Norms for tissue analyses in different growth stages, namely early budding, budding and flowering stages (1st flowering) * EB = early budding stage; B = budding stage; F = flowering stage (first flowers) In studies performed in Kansas, USA, it was found that P fertilization without K fertilization, or in the presence of insufficient K, has worse detrimental consequences for yield and survival capacity than is presumed. Lucerne yield is determined by three components, namely the spikelet or sprout mass, the number of stems per unit surface area and the number of plants per unit surface area. A yield increase will therefore be associated with an increase in any of these components. In this study it was found that the aggregate annual yield with P and K fertilization had increased, while it had not been the case with K fertilization only. The yield increase had been due to a higher spikelet mass, while the number of stems per unit surface area had made no contribution. Even though P fertilization had increased the leaf yield, plant density had decreased in that fewer but bigger lucerne plants had developed. Thus the application of only P fertilization had led to a quicker stand loss compared with areas, also the checks, that had received P and K fertilization. The loss of stand was also associated with lower levels of root reserves, including starch, amino acids and proteins. In this study the highest yield was obtained where 185 kg ha -1 and 50 kg ha -1 had been applied annually. Potassium applications without any P had shown no yield increase. Applications of 50 and 75 kg ha -1 had shown more yield increase where no K had been applied compared with the checks that had received no P or K. Without following the American data blindly, it is clear that the application of P and K must be applied together rather than separately. Studies also showed that there is interaction between soil compaction, yield, K content of tissue and K fertilization. On compacted soil K fertilization contributes more to yield than on soils that are not compacted. Guidelines for annual P and K applications as topdressing include the following: Apply immediately after harvesting, before regrowth starts. Avoid contact with wet leaves. Fertilize after first cutting to stimulate second and third regrowth, or early in April to improve winter hardiness. Avoid application if soils are loose, in order to avoid physical damage on the lucerne crown. Divide the application to prevent scorching damage if more than 500 kg ha -1 fertilizer has to be used in any year. Foliar spraying with P and K is insufficient for meeting all the needs. In other words, it cannot replace soil-applied fertilization. Foliar spraying is a good method of providing micronutrients, and to serve as starters. Apply 3 kg P ha -1 every second year for every ton of dry matter that has been removed per hectare (however, spread out between cuts). K applications of 100 kg ha -1 can be applied in spring and, if necessary, also in midsummer. This applies to hay production in particular. Potassium sulphate or potassium chloride can be considered. On sandy soils K must be applied two to three times during the season. Try to apply P and K together. However, keep in mind that the efficacy of P, applied as maintenance fertilization, is primarily determined by possible harrowing, as well as adequate moisture. As P is absorbed through diffusion and root contact, absorption of P from the topsoil surface cannot occur if that soil is dried out. Adequate watering remains critical to optimal production of lucerne. Secondary elements Calcium and Magnesium Ca and Mg deficiencies are rare, especially where the soil pH is within the acceptable parameters for lucerne cultivation. The most economical way of avoiding Ca and Mg problems, is to follow a sound liming programme, either with dolomitic and/or calcitic lime. Magnesium deficiency symptoms can manifest when soil analyses on soils with a CEC of less than 6 cmol c kg -1 soil are lower than 50 to 100 mg kg -1 . On soils with a CEC value exceeding 6 cmol c kg -1 soil, the thresholds for Mg will be higher. Magnesium deficiencies can also occur on acid, sandy soils, as well as on calcareous organic soils where large quantities of Ca in the form of calcitic lime have been applied repeatedly. If the soil pH is right and additional Mg is needed, magnesium sulphate (Epsom salts) or potassium magnesium sulphate can be considered. Sulphur Sulphur deficiencies are common where crops, such as lucerne, that need ample S, are planted on sandy soils, or on other soils that are low in organic matter, as well as soils that have not been fertilized in the previous two years. Coal, fuel oil and other fossil fuels used in industrial areas release S into the atmosphere and the sulphur then precipitates on land again. Generally speaking, sufficient S (10 to 25 kg ha -1 per annum) is released in this manner to meet the needs of crops in these areas. In areas that do not have this atmospheric advantage, S in the soil can be replenished by using fertilizer such as single superphosphate (10% S), ammonium sulphate (24% S), potassium sulphate (16% S) or gypsum (± 17% S). Because S leaches, some subsoils, particularly those that are acidic and clayey, can contain sufficient S for high-yielding crops, even if the tillage layer tests low. Where the S need has been determined, either elementary S or sulphate forms can be used on lucerne. However, it is necessary to note that sulphate sulphur is available to the crop immediately, while elemental S first has to be converted to sulphate before it can be utilised. The S guidelines are summarised in Table 5.12.2.7. Table 5.12.2.7. Sulphur fertilizer guidelines for lucerne * If elemental S is applied at this quantity, it should be adequate for the lifespan of the stand. The conversion of elemental S improves with shallow incorporation. Micro-elements Plants use micro-elements in small quantities. A shortage of any essential element will impair a plant’s growth. If an overdose of any element, but specifically micro-elements, occurs, harmful quantities in the soil can accumulate, making it difficult to rectify the problem. Soil analysis options for determining availability levels are available for most micro-elements, while there are plant analysis options available for determining the nutritional status of the plant. Boron Boron is needed for protein synthesis in lucerne and therefore it is a common micronutrient to use in a fertilizer programme. Boron uptake by lucerne is limited by both an acidic and an alkaline pH. Due to the low negative charge in sandy soil, B is readily available to the plant, while it is less accessible to plants in soil with a fine texture. Boron deficiencies can be expected if the pH deviates from neutral, otherwise such deficiencies are associated with well-drained sandy soils containing little organic matter. Do not apply B close to germinating seeds as it can kill seedlings. Apply before establishment or after they have been cut or grazed. By and large the recommendation is not to apply B more than once every three years. As a result, it is necessary to monitor B levels by means of soil analyses. Popular sources to use, are those that are boric-acid based, or to use sodium octaborate. General guidelines for B fertilization are given in Table 5.12.2.8. Table 5.12.2.8. General guidelines for boron fertilization on lucerne * Where B levels are very low, for example, 0 to 0.2, clay soils excluded, B applications can be increased by a factor of 1.5. Molybdenum Lucerne needs Mo for the initiation of nitrogen fixation. Molybdenum deficiencies only occur in acid soils. Because the availability of Mo increases with an increase in soil pH, Mo deficiencies can be eliminated through optimal liming. If Mo has to be applied, sources such as molybdic acid, sodium molybdate or ammonium molybdate can be considered. Because lucerne absorbs most nutrients from the top 200 mm of the soil, it reacts better to incorporated applications than to topdressing. Therefore Mo should be sprayed on the soil surface and worked in at 100 g ha -1 before establishment. It is essential to remedy shortages annually with a topdressing that is divided into at least two applications, namely after the first cutting and again in the middle of the season. Application occurs immediately after cutting when there is little plant material that can be burnt by the fertilizer. With all this type of traffic and in order to prevent crown damage, soils must be relatively dry before tractors are used. Foliar spray must preferably not be applied because the in-between cutting cycle of lucerne in the growing season is very short. However, where there are known shortages, 100 g ha -1 (sodium molybdate, molybdic acid or ammonium molybdate) as a spray on the crop after it has been cut can be considered. Molybdenum can poison animals, particularly if the Cu content in the plant is lower than 4 mg kg -1 and the Mo is equal to, or more than 5 mg kg -1 . Manganese, zinc, iron and copper Zinc is normally included in NPK mixes and therefore deficiency symptoms are not generally expected to be reported. However, it is advisable to do rectifications or supplements according to soil analyses. Norms according to the DTPA extraction in terms of which Zn deficiencies can be identified and treated, are given in Table 5.12.2.9. Iron deficiencies notably manifest on soils with a high free Ca content, also known as lime-induced chlorosis. Iron deficiencies also manifest on these soils particularly during cold, wet periods. Manganese and Cu deficiencies are not normally reported but deficiencies can occur. Occasionally, Mn deficiencies are observed in lower-lying areas of a field, notably where drainage is poorer or where the soil pH is significantly higher. Cu deficiencies are mainly prevalent on sandy soils. Norms according to the DTPA extraction are also given in Table 5.12.2.9. Kraal manure Kraal manure is a complete nutrient source that contains all the macro, secondary and micro-elements. It also promotes biological activity in the soil and improves the soil’s physical properties. Although kraal manure is good for the soil, it can cause problems if applied to lucerne fields. It can burn leaves and thereby reduce yield and quality. The equipment used to apply the manure can compact the soil and break plant crowns that, in turn, can reduce the yield and shorten the stand’s lifespan. Nitrogen in the kraal manure can stimulate growth of weeds and grass and weed seeds consumed by the animals can also be distributed in the lucerne. If possible, the kraal manure must be strewn on other crops that can benefit from the nitrogen. Lucerne will be able to use the applied nitrogen but, due to the N-binding capacity, does not need it. A blueprint for maximum yield In Hungary a lucerne yield of 120 t ha -1 dry matter was recorded over a 4- year period. In the corresponding period 72 t ha -1 dry roots were produced in the soil. Although 2 000 kg N ha -1 was produced through symbiotic fixation in the soil, 1 080 kg N ha -1 was applied over the 4-year period (270 kg N ha -1 per annum). The P and the K applied during the 4-year period, was 409 kg ha -1 and 1 790 kg ha -1 , respectively. Lime and Mg were also applied. All the lime and the Mg, as well as half of the total P and K, were applied in autumn, before planting. The remaining P and K were harrowed into the soil towards the end of the second year. The remaining N and micro-elements were applied by means of fertigation in three applications per annum. Irrigation was an important part of the production system and without it the yield would not have been achieved. Once again, without blindly following cases that have been recorded in other countries, lessons can be learnt, taking account of the relevant soil properties being investigated. Table 5.12.2.9. Zinc, iron, manganese and copper norms according to the DTPA extraction, together with guidelines for treatment * DTPA extraction method. ** Zinc sulphate but equal quantities of a chelate can also be used.
